data.title: Financial Independence Isn't A Finish Line
data.description:
Why chasing a number can quietly sabotage your freedom—and what to do instead

The trap of numbers

Most people chase a number when they think about financial independence. Hit $1.5 million, $2 million, or whatever target their calculators spit out, and suddenly life is supposed to feel effortless. Retire. Relax. "Made it."

The truth? That thinking is a trap. I call it the Finish Line Fallacy. The trap is deceptively simple: it treats financial independence as a milestone to cross, rather than a way of living. It convinces people that once the number is reached, the hard work is over and the outcomes are guaranteed.

Don't confuse the Finish Line Fallacy with the Arrival Fallacy. Popularized recently because of professional golfer Scottie Scheffler's press conference, the Arrival Fallacy is the idea that reaching a goal won't automatically deliver happiness or fulfillment.

But they're not the same. The Arrival Fallacy is emotional: it's about how success feels once you get there. The Finish Line Fallacy is behavioral: it's the belief that once a financial number is reached, the need for thoughtful decisions, adaptability, and discipline disappears. One leaves people disappointed. The other leaves them exposed.

The 3 failure markers

The Finish Line Fallacy leads to three subtle but persistent failure points that often go unnoticed until they collide with real life.

First, it encourages obsession with a static number rather than the systems that actually drive long-term success. Portfolio balances fluctuate. Markets dip. Life throws curveballs. If your sense of freedom and security is tied solely to a number, every surprise becomes a crisis.

Second, it fosters postponement of life in pursuit of the elusive finish line. Hobbies, travel, and experiences are deferred indefinitely, because "I'll truly enjoy life when I get there."

Third, it blinds people to the behaviors that matter most—discipline, decision quality, flexibility—because they think the outcome alone signals success. These habits can vanish under the glow of a target portfolio.

I see it all the time. One retiree reached her target portfolio at fifty-nine and expected peace of mind. Instead, the first market dip left her paralyzed, second-guessing every decision. Numbers alone hadn't delivered confidence. She had reached a "finish line" in a spreadsheet, but not in life.

The fallacy in action

People often ignore small, compounding habits that build long-term resilience: practicing monthly budget reviews, experimenting with lifestyle adjustments, or learning to tolerate short-term discomfort without panic. These daily practices matter more than a one-time milestone because they shape the way you handle uncertainty, which is the true measure of independence.

You might think you're advancing toward security, but without the right mindset, reaching a number changes nothing. The finish line becomes a mirage, always one step further ahead, while anxiety, indecision, and missed opportunities quietly accumulate.

Are you falling for it?

Spotting the Finish Line Fallacy in your own life is simpler than you might think. Notice the anxiety that lingers despite hitting a milestone. See if you postpone experiences, always waiting for "one more year" of saving or accumulation. Ask yourself whether you obsess over a number instead of focusing on the daily choices that actually create financial independence.

Sometimes recognition comes in small, quiet moments. Feeling restless despite a growing portfolio, hesitating to make a major life change even when financially feasible, or resenting someone for "being ahead" despite having fewer dollars—these are all clues. Awareness is the first step. Once the fallacy is visible, it loses its power.

Regain control

Escaping the trap isn't about complicated calculations. It's about shifting perspective. Start living with independence, not numbers. Define what freedom looks like in your daily decisions, not just in your portfolio.

Practice it long before any target is reached. Each week, take deliberate steps: review your spending on Sunday mornings—does it reflect your priorities? Pause when market news spikes anxiety and ask whether action is really necessary. Experiment with small lifestyle adjustments: a side project that grows your skills or trimming one unnecessary expense.

Even saying "no" to an impulse purchase or an overcommitted weekend strengthens the habits that build real freedom. Focus on process, not milestones. Ask yourself daily: Am I making the right choices today? Not, Have I hit the number yet?

Think of it like running a marathon. The number is just a checkpoint. Freedom comes from pacing, adapting, and living the perspective of independent decision-making at every step. Those who chase the finish line, if they arrive at all, may be exhausted, anxious, or unprepared. Those who practice independence daily move with confidence and resilience through every curveball life throws.

Decision-making under uncertainty

As I discuss in Wealth Your Way (Chapter 9), mastering your decisions, rather than obsessing over a number, is what builds durable independence.

Repetition matters. One good choice doesn't create independence. The ability to consistently make thoughtful decisions under uncertainty does. Over time, this builds what money alone can't: clarity, confidence, and real options. In other words, financial well-being.

The question that matters

Before you plan 2026, pause and ask yourself: Am I living the independence I've been chasing, or am I waiting for a number to make it real? How you answer this will shape not only the year ahead but the trajectory of your life over the next decade.

Disappointment is uncomfortable. Fragility is dangerous.

As always, invest often and wisely. Thank you for reading.

Originally published on January 16, 2026, at www.CosmoDeStefano.com. Used with permission.

My book, Wealth Your Way, is available on Amazon, and consider subscribing to my free monthly newsletter, with fresh, no-fluff insights on money, investing, and building lasting wealth.

The content provided is for informational and educational purposes only. It does not constitute legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice. You are welcome to share, quote, or use the content — including for research or machine learning — please credit Cosmo P. DeStefano and link to www.CosmoDeStefano.com.

data.title: Stock Of The Day: Is This The Bottom For Salesforce?
data.description:
Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE:CRM) shares are trading lower on Tuesday. Over the past two weeks, they have lost more than 15% of their value.

But the selloff may end soon. This is why Salesforce is the Stock of the Day.

As you can see on the chart, Salesforce has dropped to levels around $225. This price was support in August and November.

There tends to be support at former support levels because of remorseful or regretful sellers.

Some of the people who sold shares around $225 in August regretted doing so when the price rallied afterward. Many of them decided that if and when they could eventually do so, they would buy their shares back at the same price they were sold for.

As a result, when Salesforce dropped back to around $225 in November, these remorseful sellers placed buy orders. There were so many of them that it created support at the level again.

Now that the stock has returned to $225, and disappointed traders and investors who sold around this level in November will be placing buy orders. If there are enough of them, it will create support at the level again.

You can also see on the chart that the last two times Salesforce found this support, a rally followed.

Stocks rally off support when some of the buyers who created the support become anxious and impatient. They know the sellers will go to whoever is willing to pay the highest price.

So, they increase their bid prices. Other anxious and impatient sellers see this and do the same thing. This could result in a snowball effect or bidding war that can force the shares into an uptrend.

This is what happen with Salesforce the last two times it dropped to $225, and it may be about to happen again.

Photo: bluestork / Shutterstock

data.title: $1000 Invested In Credicorp 5 Years Ago Would Be Worth This Much Today
data.description:
Credicorp (NYSE:BAP) has outperformed the market over the past 5 years by 3.97% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 16.18%. Currently, Credicorp has a market capitalization of $25.56 billion.

Buying $1000 In BAP: If an investor had bought $1000 of BAP stock 5 years ago, it would be worth $2,105.33 today based on a price of $321.83 for BAP at the time of writing.

Credicorp's Performance Over Last 5 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

This article was generated by Benzinga's automated content engine and reviewed by an editor.
